sections.text: Latent Profile Analysis of Alcohol Consumption and Sexual Attitudes Among College Women: Associations With Sexual Victimization Risk

This study used latent profile analysis (LPA) to identify at-risk profiles of college freshman women (n = 481) using self-reports of alcohol consumption and sociosexuality. Analyses resulted in three profiles labeled low alcohol use–low sociosexuality, high alcohol use–medium sociosexuality, and high alcohol use–high sociosexuality. Baseline victimization predicted latent profile membership. More severely victimized women were more likely to be in the high alcohol–high sociosexuality profile than the high alcohol–medium sociosexuality and low alcohol–low sociosexuality profiles. At follow-up, the high alcohol–high sociosexuality profile had higher mean levels of victimization severity, relative to those in the high alcohol–medium sociosexuality and low alcohol–low sociosexuality profiles.

The sequelae of sexual victimization are substantial and involve both psychological and physical health problems, including problematic alcohol use and alcohol-related problems; posttraumatic stress disorder; sexual, eating, and mood disorders; risky sexual behavior; sexually transmitted infections (STIs); and rape-related pregnancies. Importantly, myriad studies have demonstrated a robust relationship between a history of victimization, either in childhood or emerging adulthood, and sexual revictimization (e.g.,), with approximately two thirds of women who report victimization being revictimized. Sexual revictimization has been linked to significant increases in risk of depression, posttraumatic stress disorder, and heavy episodic drinking, and appears to amplify the negative psychological and physical sequelae from previous assaults. At present, the strong association between victimization and revictimization is not well understood.

Sexual assault on college campuses has become an increasing focus of national concern. A and Department of Education Title IX investigations have been at the forefront of media coverage due to victim complaints that colleges have failed to respond adequately to incidents of sexual violence. Indeed, research has shown consistently that college women are a high-risk group for being victimized, and that these victimization experiences vary along a continuum of severity ranging from unwanted sexual contact to completed rape. Specifically, approximately 50% of college women have experienced some form of sexual violence, with 25% of those women experiencing an attempted or completed rape during their college years. This research suggests also that the yearly incidence of sexual assault is higher among college women than women of a comparable age in the general population.

In spite of the development of numerous preventive interventions aimed at decreasing the prevalence of sexual violence on college campuses in recent years, rates of victimization have remained relatively steady over several decades (see, for comparison,). Moreover, these interventions, although reasonably effective at changing attitudes, have been relatively ineffective at changing behavior and have been implemented often as static, one-time risk-reduction interventions with limited focus on specific groups of at-risk women. There also is a paucity of effective interventions to reduce men’s sexually aggressive behavior, and, as a consequence, research focused on augmenting women’s ability to reduce their risk of victimization remains a necessary adjunct to work on men’s sexual aggression. To date, only a handful of programs have shown promise in reducing rates of victimization among college women (e.g.,), and the majority have been ineffective at preventing assault among at-risk women, that is, previously victimized women.

One strategy for improving sexual assault programs may be to tailor them to specific groups of women. This may be particularly important for at-risk women, as the mechanisms responsible for revictimization still require further illumination. Person-centered analyses, such as latent profile analysis (LPA), are one approach used to empirically derive profiles of individuals with similar responses to a set of measures. Analyses such as LPA (and relatedly, latent class analysis [LCA]) can assist in identifying groups of women who share similarities with respect to victimization risk and who are different from women who are at lower risk of victimization. The assumption of this type of person-centered analysis is that profiles reflect important configurations (, as cited in), namely, that the associations among the measured variables are explained by some underlying categorical latent factor that provides information about likely group membership. LPA and similar latent variable analytic techniques may be particularly relevant to the study of sexual victimization risk in that they can inform the development of preventive interventions for specific groups of at-risk women. Moreover, these approaches may also help deepen our understanding of the processes linked to sexual victimization and revictimization. As an example, LPA was used in previous work to obtain four profile groups of at-risk college women based on their previous victimization, alcohol use, relationship expectancies of the perpetrator, and precautionary behaviors. These profiles then were used to examine variability among them for responding to a sexual assault based on cognitive, emotional, and behavioral factors. Women in these profiles differed in their responses to sexual assault, and the findings had implications for tailored sexual assault resistance programming.

Two factors that may be especially important for identifying groups of women at high risk of sexual victimization are alcohol use and sociosexual attitudes (i.e., sociosexuality). Clearly, alcohol use is intimately intertwined with sexual assault, with approximately 50% of assaults among college women involving alcohol use. Longitudinal research has shown that alcohol use, heavy episodic drinking, and exposure to heavy drinking contexts, all common experiences among college women, heighten their risk of sexual assault. Alcohol use also is thought to increase incidents of casual sex, encounters that are becoming increasingly common among college students and are thought to increase women’s risk of sexual assault. Related research also has found that women who experienced verbal sexual coercion reported more sexual partners, stronger sex-related alcohol expectancies (i.e., the belief alcohol will enhance sexual behavior), and more frequent alcohol use prior to engaging in sexual intercourse. Conceptual frameworks such as alcohol myopia theory and the appraisal–disruption model of stress responding have been used to explain how alcohol might increase women’s victimization risk by impairing their ability to perceive and respond to social situations. Importantly, findings from alcohol challenge studies suggest that alcohol may increase the likelihood of victimization by disrupting women’s capacity to detect risk, as well as their ability to resist unwanted sexual advances.

Sociosexuality is conceptualized as an individual’s orientation toward and participation in uncommitted, casual sexual relationships in the absence of emotional closeness. It is composed of both sociosexual behavior (i.e., engaging in uncommitted sex regularly), sociosexual attitudes (i.e., one’s appraisals or evaluations on engaging in such behavior), and sexual desire (i.e., desire/interest in someone who is not a committed sexual partner). Persons high in sociosexuality are considered to be “unrestricted,” and interested in participating in casual sex with others, whereas persons low in sociosexuality are more “restricted” and show less interest in such sexual interactions. In general, sociosexuality is thought to be a reasonably stable pattern of behavior (i.e., trait like) that also includes specific attitudinal features. Sociosexuality has also been conceptualized historically as a construct involving one dimension, and although recent work has examined the utility of distinguishing between sociosexual attitudes and behaviors, the relationship between these attitudes and behaviors appears stronger for women than for men (e.g.,). It is thought that women who have “unrestricted” attitudes do not experience difficulty making their sexual behaviors align with their attitudes, as women, relative to men, can readily access willing sexual partners.

Notably, a handful of studies with college women have found an association between sociosexuality and women’s judgments of victimization risk. In this work, women higher in sociosexuality, relative to women lower in sociosexuality, judged victimization risk to be lower in hypothetical dating and social situations. In related work, sociosexuality mediated the relationship between victimization history and responses to similar social situations, with women who reported more severe victimization histories also reporting higher sociosexuality, which, in turn, was associated with responses judged by sexual violence research experts to be less effective in decreasing victimization risk. In addition, in a path analytic study exploring relationships among ethnicity, sexual attitudes, number of lifetime sexual partners, child sexual abuse, and severity of adolescent/adult sexual assault, European American women, as compared with Hispanic women, reported more severe adolescent/adult victimization, which was explained by higher sociosexuality and more lifetime sexual partners. In a recent alcohol challenge study, higher sociosexuality was associated with higher thresholds for judging social situations as risky (i.e., high sociosexuality women required more evidence of risk prior to making a high-risk classification). Sociosexuality also interacted with condition (i.e., alcohol vs. no alcohol), such that higher sociosexuality was associated with lower sensitivity to risk information when making explicit risk judgments in the alcohol condition but not in the no-alcohol condition. Finally, in related work, sociosexual attitudes among college men and women were indirectly related to alcohol use through sociosexual behavior. Although not measured directly, engagement in such behavior would presumably heighten risk of adverse events, including sexual assault.

Overall then, alcohol use and sociosexuality appear to be important factors in understanding women’s victimization risk. The focus of the current study was to use LPA to identify profiles based on women’s self-reported alcohol use and sociosexuality, and examine the relationship among these profiles and baseline and follow-up sexual victimization. Study participants were college freshman women who were part of a larger prospective study examining whether risk processing difficulties predicted severity of victimization at 6 months, as well as whether these difficulties mediated the association between prior victimization and the severity of future victimization. Given the relative paucity of work in this area, we did not make specific predictions about what profiles might emerge from the data. However, we did expect that women who reported the highest levels of alcohol use and sociosexuality would report higher levels of baseline and follow-up victimization rates.
Method
Participants

Participants were 481 freshman women at a medium-sized, Southwestern university. Mean age was 18.20 years (SD = 0.53 years). With respect to marital status, 480 participants reported being single (99.8%); only one participant reported cohabitating with a romantic partner (0.2%). The majority of the women identified as heterosexual (88.8%, n = 427), with 11.2% (n = 54) identifying as bisexual. The sample was ethnically diverse, with 39.5% (n = 190) White/Caucasian, 36.4% (n = 175) Hispanic American, 6.9% (n = 33) Mexican American, 6.0% (n = 29) Asian/Pacific Islander, 4.8% (n = 23) African American, 2.5% (n = 12) American Indian/Alaskan Native, and 4.0% (n = 19) “Other.”
Self-Report Measures
Demographics Questionnaire.
This self-report measure asked participants for their age, marital status, sexual orientation, racial/ethnic membership, and academic status.
Sexual Experiences Survey (SES).

The SES is a 10-item self-report questionnaire developed to measure degrees of severity of sexual victimization (unwanted sexual contact to completed rape) since the age of 14. The SES uses behaviorally specific definitions of sexual assault and asks participants to indicate whether the event occurred by choosing one of two dichotomous response options (i.e., no or yes). reported that the SES had an internal consistency of α = .74, a 1-week test– retest reliability of r = .93, and a correlation of r = .73 with interview responses, suggesting that the SES is a reasonable measure of self-reported sexual victimization. The SES was used at baseline to capture sexual victimization experiences since the age of 14; at follow-up, the SES was used again to measure sexual victimization experiences since the baseline assessment, and the instructional set on the questionnaire was altered to reflect this time frame.

Consistent with a dimensional conceptualization of sexual victimization, as well as prior work using the SES (e.g.,), participants were assigned to the severity category based on the most severe victimization experience they reported having since the age of 14. Sexual victimization was coded as 0 = no unwanted experiences, 1 = unwanted sexual contact, 2 = sexual coercion, 3 = attempted rape, and 4 = rape. With respect to participants’ sexual victimization experiences since the age of 14, 37.4% (n = 180) of participants reported no history of sexual victimization, 18.1% (n = 87) reported unwanted sexual contact, 23.9% (n = 115) reported sexual coercion, 4.6% (n = 22) reported attempted rape, and 16.0% (n = 77) reported rape. At follow-up, 52.5% (n = 238) reported no sexual victimization since baseline assessment, 16.6% (n = 75) reported unwanted sexual contact, 18.5% (n = 84) reported sexual coercion, 2.9% (n = 13) reported attempted rape, and 9.5% (n = 43) reported rape. Of the 453 women who completed both baseline and follow-up assessments, 29.36% (n = 133) reported no sexual victimization, 105 (23.18%) reported baseline-only sexual victimization, 178 (39.29%) reported sexual victimization at both baseline and follow-up, and 37 (8.17%) reported a first incident of sexual victimization. Thus, the majority of women reporting victimization at follow-up had experienced a previous act of sexual violence.
Revised Sociosexual Orientation Inventory (SOI-R).

The SOI-R is a nine-item questionnaire designed to measure willingness to engage in uncommitted sexual relationships. Items represent distinct aspects of sociosexuality that show low to moderate intercorrelations (.17-.55). Items are summed for a total score of global sociosexuality. In the current sample, the internal consistency of the SOI-R was .84. The SOI-R items are included in Figure 1 and Table 2 notes.
Quantity and frequency of alcohol consumption (Q-F).

Alcohol quantity and frequency of use were evaluated with a variation of the measure used by and Bryan and colleagues in their work. Participants rated their (a) frequency of use in the last 3 months, (b) typical quantity of drinks in one sitting, and (c) frequency of getting drunk when drinking in the past 6 months. Items can be examined separately or standardized and averaged into a highly reliable scale (i.e., α = .87). The Q-F items are included in the Figure 1 and Table 2 notes.
Procedure

The study was conducted in compliance with the university’s internal review board. Participants were recruited through freshman orientation and email blasts that were sent to freshman women. The study was described as one that was examining college women’s perceptions of and responses to dating and social situations. Inclusion criteria for participating were being a freshman, 18–24 years old, unmarried, and heterosexual or bisexual. Because the stimuli used in the tasks that were part of the larger study depict situations that unmarried, heterosexual, or bisexual undergraduate women between the ages 18 and 24 might face when dating or interacting socially with men, women who were married, lesbian, or above the age of 24 were not eligible to participate. Participants were compensated US$20 for baseline and US$40 for 6-month follow-up assessments, and they completed all tasks and questionnaires in a private assessment room on computers using Opinio, an online survey program. At baseline, participants completed all tasks and self-report questionnaires; at 6-month follow-up, participants again completed all baseline tasks and self-report questionnaires on the computer, except for the demographics form.
Statistical Analyses

All variables were examined for missing values, normality of distributions, and outliers. LPA was used to estimate profiles of drinking and sociosexuality within this female college student population. There were 481 cases used in the LPAs and all models were analyzed using the MPlus 7.4 software package (Muthén & Muthén, 1998–2010). Two parameters in the latent profile model are of greatest interest: (a) latent profile prevalence indicates how many people are expected to be classified in each profile and (b) individual item means, given a particular latent profile membership. The following fit indices were used to decide on the number of classes: Akaike information criterion (AIC), Bayesian information criterion (BIC), adjusted Bayesian information criterion (aBIC), and the Lo–Mendell–Rubin adjusted likelihood ratio test (LRT).

For the AIC, BIC, and aBIC, lower values indicated a better fitting model. The LRT was used to compare models with increasing number of classes, whereby a significant LRT indicated a k class model provided a significantly better fit than a k − 1 class model. Relative entropy was also used to examine the classification precision of the model, with entropy values greater than 0.80 indicating adequate classification precision.

After deciding on the number of profiles in a model without covariates (i.e., an unconditional model), we tested two models with covariates (i.e., conditional models). First, we incorporated baseline sexual victimization history as a predictor of profile membership using a model-based approach with multinomial logistic regression of profiles regressed on sexual victimization history. Second, we examined whether there were differences in the latent profiles on follow-up sexual victimization by examining follow-up sexual victimization as a distal outcome using the Bolck, Croon, and Hagenaars (BCH; 2004) method. The distal outcome analysis provided differences in mean sexual victimization at follow-up across profiles using a Wald chi-square test, controlling for baseline sexual victimization as a predictor of latent profile membership.
Results
Unconditional Latent Profile Models

A two-profile model was computed initially and subsequent models including additional profiles (up to five profiles) were computed to compare AIC, BIC, aBIC, entropy, LRT, and Bootstrap Likelihood Ratio Test (BLRT) to decide on the best fitting model. Based on the fit indices listed in Table 1, we selected the three-profile model as providing the best fit to the data. Specifically, the AIC, BIC, and aBIC all decreased from the two-profile model to the three-profile model. In addition, the LRT indicated the three-profile model fit significantly better than the two-profile model, and a four-profile model did not fit significantly better than the three-profile model. Entropy for the three-profile model was 0.93, suggesting good classification precision.

Based on the latent profile prevalence, Profile 1 included approximately 238 participants (49.5%), Profile 2 included approximately 169 participants (35.4%), and Profile 3 included approximately 74 participants (15.2%). To meaningfully interpret each profile, the estimated means on each item within each profile were compared with the overall sample means (see Table 2). Sociosexuality estimated item means were higher in Profile 3 compared with the sample means and the Profile 1 and Profile 2 estimated means, suggesting that Profile 3 includes individuals who are reporting higher sociosexuality, on average, than Profiles 1 and 2. Sociosexuality estimated item means were lower in Profile 1 compared with the sample means and estimated means of Profiles 2 and 3, suggesting that these women are reporting lower sociosexuality, on average, than individuals expected to be in Profiles 2 and 3. Sociosexuality item means in Profile 2 were comparable with the sample means of the group, suggesting that these women were reporting average or “medium” sociosexuality. Quantity and frequency of alcohol consumption estimated item means were higher in both Profiles 2 and 3, as compared with the sample means of Profile 1, suggesting that individuals expected to be in Profiles 2 and 3 are reporting higher rates of alcohol consumption than individuals expected to be in Profile 1.

Due to the consistent trend of Profile 1 in engaging in lower alcohol use and reporting lower sociosexuality relative to Profiles 2 and 3, we labeled this profile as the low alcohol–low sociosexuality profile. Due to the consistent pattern of Profile 2 engaging in higher alcohol use and reporting average rates of sociosexuality relative to Profiles 1 and 3, we called Profile 2 the high alcohol–medium sociosexuality profile. Finally, due to the consistent trend of Profile 3 engaging in higher alcohol use and reporting higher sociosexuality, as compared with Profiles 1 and 2, we labeled this profile the high alcohol–high sociosexuality profile.
Conditional Latent Profile Models

Using the three-profile solution, described above, we incorporated sexual victimization history at baseline as a predictor of profile membership. The entropy of the model was unchanged, relatively entropy = 0.93, and the LRT indicated the three-profile model still fit significantly better than a two-profile model (LRT = 691.58, p = .0062). As shown in Figure 1, the profile prevalence for the three-profile model and individual item means within each profile were similar to the unconditional model. Profile 1 (approximately 48.7% of the sample) reported lower average alcohol consumption and sociosexuality. Profile 2 (approximately 35.5% of the sample) reported high average alcohol consumption and average levels of sociosexuality. Profile 3 (approximately 15.7% of the sample) reported high average alcohol consumption and high sociosexuality. Baseline sexual victimization was a significant predictor of expected membership in each of the latent profiles using a multinomial logistic regression. Specifically, as compared with the high alcohol–high sociosexuality profile (Profile 3 as the reference profile), individuals with more severe baseline sexual victimization were significantly less likely to be in the low alcohol–low sociosexuality profile, odds ratio (OR) = 0.37, B (SE) = −1.00 (0.12), p < .001, and the high alcohol–medium sociosexuality profile, OR = 0.57, B (SE) = −0.57 (0.11), p < .001. Similarly, as compared with the high alcohol–medium sociosexuality profile (Profile 2 as the reference profile), individuals with more severe baseline sexual victimization were significantly less likely to be in the low alcohol–low sociosexuality profile, OR = 0.65, B (SE) = −0.43 (0.10), p < .001.

Finally, we examined whether experiences of follow-up sexual victimization varied by latent profiles, after controlling for baseline sexual victimization history, using a distal outcomes analysis in Mplus. As shown in Figure 2, results indicated that individuals in the high alcohol–high sociosexuality profile had significantly higher mean levels of victimization severity at follow-up, as compared with those in the high alcohol–medium sociosexuality profile, Wald χ2(1) = 19.66, p < .001, and as compared with those in the low alcohol–low sociosexuality profile, Wald χ2(1) = 45.67, p < .001. Individuals in the high alcohol–medium sociosexuality profile also had significantly higher mean levels of victimization severity at follow-up, as compared with those in the low alcohol–low sociosexuality profile, Wald χ2(1) = 10.02, p = .002.
Discussion

College women are a high-risk group for sexual victimization, an experience that is associated with numerous negative psychological and physical health outcomes, including risk of sexual revictimization. Despite interventions developed over the years to decrease rates of sexual assault, the prevalence of victimization on college campuses has remained relatively steady. Moreover, extant interventions have often been implemented as “one size fits all” approaches (e.g.,) and have been reasonably effective at changing attitudes but less effective at reducing rates of victimization, particularly among at-risk women. One approach for improving these programs, proposed here and by previous authors (e.g.,), is to tailor preventive interventions to specific groups of high-risk women. Person-centered analyses (LCA, LPA) are one method for identifying such groups of women. Because alcohol use (e.g.,) and sociosexuality (e.g.,) have been associated with increased risk of sexual victimization, we chose these two factors to obtain profiles among our sample of freshman women. We then examined the association among these profiles and victimization severity at both baseline and 6-month follow-up. Our findings extend the extant literature by identifying particular groups of women who may be at higher or lower risk of sexual victimization. Replication of these profiles is needed in future work; nonetheless, these findings provide additional support for a person-centered analytic approach in classifying college women who may be especially vulnerable to sexual violence.

The fit indices of our LPA analyses supported three distinct profiles and indicated that three profiles fit the data better than either two or four profiles. The estimated means for each item within each profile, relative to the overall sample means, helped facilitate an interpretation of each profile. Women expected to be classified in Profile 1 reported lower alcohol use and lower sociosexuality than Profiles 2 and 3. Women expected to be classified in Profile 2 reported higher alcohol use than Profile 1 and average levels of sociosexuality. Finally, women expected to be classified in Profile 3 reported the highest levels of alcohol use and sociosexuality, relative to Profiles 1 and 2.

With respect to specific profiles, women reporting more severe victimization at baseline were significantly less likely to be in the low alcohol–low sociosexuality and the high alcohol–medium sociosexuality profiles. More severely victimized women also were less likely to be in the low alcohol–low sociosexuality profile relative to women in the high alcohol–medium sociosexuality profile. Importantly, women in the high alcohol–high sociosexuality profile reported significantly higher mean levels of victimization severity at 6-month follow-up relative to the high alcohol–medium sociosexuality and the low alcohol–low sociosexuality profiles. Women in the high alcohol–low sociosexuality profile also reported higher mean levels of victimization severity at follow-up as compared with the low alcohol–low sociosexuality profile.

Why might women who concurrently have higher levels of alcohol use and sociosexuality be at heightened risk of sexual victimization? Clearly, prior work points to each factor, separately, as being associated with increased victimization risk (e.g.,). Sociosexuality is shown to be related to two personality traits, namely, extroversion and lack of constraint. Thus, these response tendencies, in concert with the disinhibitory effects of alcohol, may provide one potential explanation for these findings. Prior alcohol administration research supports this possibility. For instance, higher sociosexuality women relied less on risk-relevant information when making explicit judgments of victimization risk when under the influence of alcohol. However, when in a no-alcohol condition, higher sociosexuality women, as compared with lower sociosexuality women, demonstrated somewhat higher reliance on the same information. This makes theoretical sense, as women higher in sociosexuality would likely have greater experience with situations involving alcohol use and sexual behavior, thereby enhancing these women’s accuracy in detecting risk cues. However, once intoxicated, this apparent skill in risk detection might dissipate, consequently increasing these women’s risk of victimization.

In the same study, lower sociosexuality women relied on risk cues to a similar degree, regardless of whether they received alcohol or no alcohol. Thus, these women, when drinking, may rely more heavily on risk cues, and when combined with lower sociosexuality, may be less likely to enter or remain in situations that are at high risk of victimization, thereby reducing their risk. Clearly, women in the current study who reported more severe baseline victimization were considerably less likely to be in the low alcohol–low sociosexuality profile and were more likely to report higher follow-up victimization rates. Overall then, sociosexuality may act like a “behavioral disinhibitor,” and in combination with the pharmacological effects of alcohol, may heighten women’s risk of experiencing adverse events, including sexual assault.

Related research also supports the view that extroverted or impulsive response tendencies, in conjunction with alcohol, may increase college women’s victimization risk. In this work, significant interactions were found between the presence of alcohol (alcohol present vs. absent) in hypothetical risky social situations and participants’ reported level of disinhibition and their number of lifetime sexual partners. Specifically, for women reporting higher disinhibition and more sexual partners, the presence of alcohol was related to the selection of more acquiescent responses to the situations. In contrast, for women reporting lower disinhibition and fewer sexual partners, the presence or absence of alcohol did not significantly affect how they chose to respond to the situations. Remarkably, even without the pharmacological effects of alcohol, women reporting a greater tendency toward disinhibited behavior demonstrated response difficulties that may make them more vulnerable to sexual assault in real-life situations.

What might be the implications for harm-reduction programs for these at-risk women? Prior work found that women trained to attend to risk cues in a learning task (i.e., given multiple trials with feedback) relied more on these cues when making determinations of victimization risk. Notably, this effect was especially robust for at-risk women, that is, women who reported a more severe victimization history and higher rape myth acceptance, suggesting that cognitive training programs might also be useful for college women reporting greater alcohol use and higher sociosexuality. These programs could provide explicit instruction on victimization risk cues, trial-by-trial feedback on risk judgments, and block-by-block feedback on the accuracy of their risk judgments. Similar paradigms could be developed to enhance the effectiveness of women’s responses to high-risk situations. Importantly, alcohol administration could co-occur with the use of such paradigms; women could complete these tasks when intoxicated and again when sober, receiving feedback about their performance under both conditions. Related work using personalized feedback to reduce college women’s alcohol use and increase their safer sex practices has shown promise in producing behavior change. Of course, these are just a few ideas that diverge from common, static, and “one size fits all” interventions. Many more approaches are likely possible.

The present study had several notable strengths, including the aim of identifying specific, at-risk groups of college women, and the examination prospectively of the relevance of these profiles with respect to women’s risk of victimization. In addition to replicating these profiles in future work, it may also be important to assess past history of childhood sexual abuse (CSA), as prior work shows a rather robust association between CSA and adolescent/adult sexual victimization (e.g.,). We did not assess CSA in the current work, and women who report both CSA and adolescent/adult sexual assault (ASA) might be expected to fall into a different profile than women who have experienced only one victimization type.

In addition, we did not assess sexual risk behaviors (e.g., condom use) directly, although the first three items of the SOI measure sexual behaviors associated with casual sex, which is clearly linked to sexual victimization, STIs, and unintended pregnancies. Notably, prior work has also shown that sociosexuality is associated with unprotected sex and STIs. Moreover, research also indicates that women who have experienced sexual assault engaged in high rates of unprotected sex, which increased their risk of STIs. Alcohol use also is thought to decrease condom use and increase incidents of casual sex and risk of STIs. Because we did not assess sexual risk behaviors, or participation in these behaviors in concert with alcohol use, we cannot determine whether sociosexuality alone heightened women’s risk of victimization, or whether sociosexuality was simply a proxy for high-risk sexual behavior that was more closely linked to assault risk. Future work might profitably disentangle these relationships.

Finally, we also cannot conclusively determine whether the observed associations with sociosexuality are being “driven” by it, or by a related construct, such as extroversion or disinhibition. Future work employing multiple related measures in addition to sociosexuality would help illuminate which construct produces the largest effects in relation to victimization experiences among college women. If sociosexuality is uniquely important in understanding victimization, it should still show unique variance in assault outcomes (even if attenuated) when controlling for related constructs, such as disinhibition or extroversion.

It is a sad fact that sexual violence against women, ranging from unwanted sexual contact to completed rape, remains an intractable problem in our society. The remedy for the epidemic of sexual assault on college campuses remains elusive, and to date, more work remains to be done to identify risk-reduction programs for the most vulnerable college women. It is our hope that research that attempts to identify groups of at-risk women may inform the development of risk-reduction strategies that will be effective at reducing rates of sexual violence among women on college campuses.
Declaration of Conflicting Interests
The author(s) declared no potential conflicts of interest with respect to the research, authorship, and/or publication of this article.

Note. QF01 = Quantity–Frequency Item 1 (In the last 3 months, how often did you consume at least one alcoholic drink?); QF02 = Quantity–Frequency Item 2 (In the last 3 months, how many drinks did you usually have at one time?); QF03 = Quantity–Frequency Item 3 (In the last 3 months, when you drank alcohol how often did you get drunk?); SOI01 = Sociosexuality Item 1 (With how many different partners have you had sex within the past 12 months?); SOI02 = Sociosexuality Item 2 (With how many different partners have you had sexual intercourse on one and only one occasion?); SOI03 = Sociosexuality Item 3 (With how many different partners have you had sexual intercourse without having an interest in a long-term committed relationship with this person?); SOI04 = Sociosexuality Item 4 (Sex without love is OK); SOI05 = Sociosexuality Item 5 (I can imagine myself being comfortable and enjoying “casual” sex with different partners); SOI06 = Sociosexuality Item 6 (I do not want to have sex with a person until I am sure that we will have a long-term, serious relationship [reverse scored]); SOI07 = Sociosexuality Item 7 (How often do you have fantasies about having sex with someone you are not in a committed romantic relationship with?); SOI08 = Sociosexuality Item 8 (How often do you experience sexual arousal when you are in contact with someone you are not in a committed romantic relationship with?); SOI09 = Sociosexuality Item 9 (In everyday life, how often do you have spontaneous fantasies about having sex with someone you have just met?).
Distal outcomes analysis, average follow-up victimization by latent profiles.
Fit Indices for Unconditional Latent Profile Models.
Note. AIC = Akaike information criterion; BIC = Bayesian information criterion; aBIC = adjusted Bayesian information criterion; LRT = likelihood ratio test.
